text:
There, by the forge, and there beside the anvil,  
Look on your iron-hearted smiths as they lift their hammers,  
Overhand with such calm, overhand they rise and strike, ringing down in glad metallic crash,  
Like a burst of laughter.
source_text:
There by the furnace, and there by the anvil,
Behold thy sturdy blacksmiths swinging their sledges,
Overhand so steady, overhand they turn and fall with joyous clank,
Like a tumult of laughter.

text:
PURGANAX:
The future stands as black as death, a cloud,
As dark as Hell’s own scowl, suspended there—
The troops grow mutinous, the revenues fail—
There’s something foul within us—for the plain
Of the State tilts, its very foundations cave,
And the most daring turn away from themselves!
source_text:
PURGANAX:
The future looks as black as death, a cloud,
Dark as the frown of Hell, hangs over it—
The troops grow mutinous—the revenue fails—
There’s something rotten in us—for the level _100
Of the State slopes, its very bases topple,
The boldest turn their backs upon themselves!

text:
The crying child could not be heard,  
The grieving parents wept in vain:  
They stripped him to his tiny shirt,  
And fastened him in iron chain,
source_text:
The weeping child could not be heard,
The weeping parents wept in vain:
They stripped him to his little shirt,
And bound him in an iron chain,

text:
I sing to fill the waiting,
My bonnet to just tie,
And close the door to my little house;
No more is left to try,
source_text:
I sing to use the waiting,
My bonnet but to tie,
And shut the door unto my house;
No more to do have I,

text:
Of pounding feet? of laughter? of the cry, the scream,
Of joy too vast to hold? Look there! Hark!
They come, they come! Make way! Yet you misdream—
It is but a crowd of maniacs, gaunt and stark,
Driven like a band of ghosts through the dark,
From the choked well where up a bright death-fire sprang,
A lurid earth-star, shedding many a spark
From its blue trailing flame, that widely clung
To their wild hair, like mist that crowns the highest pines among.
source_text:
12.
‘Of rushing feet? laughter? the shout, the scream,
Of triumph not to be contained? See! hark! _4325
They come, they come! give way!’ Alas, ye deem
Falsely—’tis but a crowd of maniacs stark
Driven, like a troop of spectres, through the dark,
From the choked well, whence a bright death-fire sprung,
A lurid earth-star, which dropped many a spark _4330
From its blue train, and spreading widely, clung
To their wild hair, like mist the topmost pines among.

text:
“I have no name;
I am only two days old.”
What name shall I give you?
“I am happy,
Joy is my name.”
May sweet joy come upon you!
source_text:
‘I have no name;
I am but two days old.’
What shall I call thee?
‘I happy am,
Joy is my name.’
Sweet joy befall thee!

text:
“Spirit,” the Fairy said,  
and gestured toward the splendid dome,  
“This is a marvel to behold,  
and puts all mortal grandeur to shame;  
yet if virtue’s only prize were to abide  
in some celestial palace, wholly given  
to pleasure’s drift, sealed up  
within the soul’s own prison, then the will  
of steadfast Nature would remain unfilled.  
Learn to make others happy. Spirit, come!  
This is your lofty reward: the past shall rise;  
you shall behold the present; I will teach  
the hidden secrets of the future.”
source_text:
‘Spirit!’ the Fairy said, _55
And pointed to the gorgeous dome,
‘This is a wondrous sight
And mocks all human grandeur;
But, were it virtue’s only meed, to dwell
In a celestial palace, all resigned _60
To pleasurable impulses, immured
Within the prison of itself, the will
Of changeless Nature would be unfulfilled.
Learn to make others happy. Spirit, come!
This is thine high reward:—the past shall rise; _65
Thou shalt behold the present; I will teach
The secrets of the future.’

text:
Land, bearing everything, receiving all—not only the good, all that is good  
for you,  
Land, in God’s domains, become a domain for yourself,  
Under God’s governance, become a governance for yourself.
source_text:
6
Land tolerating all, accepting all, not for the good alone, all good
for thee,
Land in the realms of God to be a realm unto thyself,
Under the rule of God to be a rule unto thyself.

text:
Pity would vanish away  
If we never made another one poor,  
And mercy could no longer stay  
If all were as glad as we.
source_text:
Pity would be no more
If we did not make somebody poor,
And Mercy no more could be
If all were as happy as we.

text:
With heedless tread I wandered on,  
My heart made glad by nature’s cheer;  
Then, musing in a lonesome glen,  
A lovely maid I chanced to spy:  
Her face was like the morning’s eye,  
Her grace like spring’s own sunny smile;  
And perfection, pausing as it passed,  
Said, “Mark the lass o’ Ballochmyle!”
source_text:
With careless step I onward stray’d,
My heart rejoic’d in nature’s joy,
When, musing in a lonely glade,
A maiden fair I chanc’d to spy:
Her look was like the morning’s eye,
Her air like nature’s vernal smile:
Perfection whisper’d, passing by,
“Behold the lass o’ Ballochmyle!”

text:
For you, no child of stall and byre,
Who sweetly strike the Scottish lyre,
My thanks for every line:
The dappled plaid you kindly spare,
I’d keep with care and grateful fare;
It would delight me fine.

I’d rather, proud of fortune’s grace,
Have it hung soft o’er my shoulder,
Than any ermine’s stately place,
Or purple worn by ruler.

Farewell then, live long and well,
And plenty fill your hall;
May losses and afflictions
Ne’er call at your threshold at all!
source_text:
For you, no bred to barn and byre,
Wha sweetly tune the Scottish lyre,
Thanks to you for your line:
The marled plaid ye kindly spare,
By me should gratefully be ware;
’Twad please me to the nine.
I’d be mair vauntie o’ my hap,
Douce hingin owre my curple,
Than ony ermine ever lap,
Or proud imperial purple.
Farewell then, lang hale then,
An’ plenty be your fa;
May losses and crosses
Ne’er at your hallan ca’!

text:
Without ending can Liberty be, and without turning back, Equality;  
They dwell in the hearts of young men and the finest women,  
(Not for nothing have the earth’s unbending heads always been  
eager to fall before Liberty.)
source_text:
Without extinction is Liberty, without retrograde is Equality,
They live in the feelings of young men and the best women,
(Not for nothing have the indomitable heads of the earth been always
ready to fall for Liberty.)

text:
Peter is a many-sided Peter, a Peter cut with countless faces. He shifts  
his colors like a chameleon, his coat like a serpent’s skin. He is a Proteus  
of a Peter. At first he seemed sublime, moving, imposing, deep;  
then merely flat; then wordy and flat; and now flat—oh, so terribly flat! it is  
an ultra-legitimate flatness.
source_text:
Peter is a polyhedric Peter, or a Peter with many sides. He changes
colours like a chameleon, and his coat like a snake. He is a Proteus
of a Peter. He was at first sublime, pathetic, impressive, profound;
then dull; then prosy and dull; and now dull—oh so very dull! it is
an ultra-legitimate dulness.

text:
And the wand-thin lily, rising up
Like a Maenad, with its moonlit cup,
Until the blazing star, that serves as eye,
Looked through the dew at the tender sky;
source_text:
And the wand-like lily, which lifted up,
As a Maenad, its moonlight-coloured cup,
Till the fiery star, which is its eye,
Gazed through clear dew on the tender sky; _35

text:
O such themes—equalities! O sacred middle!
Warblings beneath the sun, arriving now, at noon, or in the falling light,
Musical strains drifting through the ages, reaching here at last,
I take up your wild, mingled chords, add my own, and
gladly carry them onward.
source_text:
O such themes--equalities! O divine average!
Warblings under the sun, usher’d as now, or at noon, or setting,
Strains musical flowing through ages, now reaching hither,
I take to your reckless and composite chords, add to them, and
cheerfully pass them forward.

text:
The climbing moon began to shine  
Above the far Cumnock hills divine:  
To count her horns, with all my might,  
I tried my skill;  
But whether she had three or four  
I could not tell.
source_text:
The rising moon began to glowre
The distant Cumnock hills out-owre:
To count her horns, wi’ a my pow’r,
I set mysel’;
But whether she had three or four,
I cou’d na tell.

text:
All the earth and air
Grow loud with your voice,
As when, through the bare night,
From a single, lonely cloud
The moon pours down her light, and heaven runs over.
source_text:
All the earth and air
With thy voice is loud,
As, when night is bare,
From one lonely cloud
The moon rains out her beams, and Heaven is overflowed. _30

text:
2.
Let stars cast down their light, but not their love on me,
for in me love has always
made health a mound of embers, quick to die away—
source_text:
2.
Send the stars light, but send not love to me,
In whom love ever made
Health like a heap of embers soon to fade—

text:
O ripened joy of womanhood! O happiness come at last!
I am more than eighty years old, the most honored mother,
How bright my mind has grown—how all people come near to me!
What is this allure beyond any known before? What bloom
outshines the bloom of youth?
What beauty is this that settles upon me and rises from within me?
source_text:
O ripen’d joy of womanhood! O happiness at last!
I am more than eighty years of age, I am the most venerable mother,
How clear is my mind--how all people draw nigh to me!
What attractions are these beyond any before? what bloom more
than the bloom of youth?
What beauty is this that descends upon me and rises out of me?

text:
[Footnote 2: Another preacher, dear to the small company as much,
who at that time was suffering ill health. For him, see also “The Ordination,”
stanza ix.—R.B.]
source_text:
[Footnote 2: Another preacher, an equal favourite with the few,
who was at that time ailing. For him see also “The Ordination,”
stanza ix.—R.B.]

text:
Raising the thunder of their praise,
then toward the City moved the crowd,
and I with them, went up in joy—a nation
set free by love; a mighty fellowship
bound by a vigilant exchange of good;
a radiant pageant, more magnificent
than kings’ slaves, jeweled in gold and blood,
when they come back from slaughter and are led
in blazing triumph beneath the crowded battlements.
source_text:
14.
Lifting the thunder of their acclamation,
Towards the City then the multitude,
And I among them, went in joy—a nation
Made free by love;—a mighty brotherhood _1840
Linked by a jealous interchange of good;
A glorious pageant, more magnificent
Than kingly slaves arrayed in gold and blood,
When they return from carnage, and are sent
In triumph bright beneath the populous battlement. _1845

text:
Has ancient Kilmarnock looked on the fiend,
Or great Mackinlay^1 once more turned his heel awry?
Or Robertson^2 again grown whole and sound,
To preach and read?
“Not worse than all!” each fellow cries,
“Tam Samson’s dead!”
source_text:
Has auld Kilmarnock seen the deil?
Or great Mackinlay^1 thrawn his heel?
Or Robertson^2 again grown weel,
To preach an’ read?
“Na’ waur than a’!” cries ilka chiel,
“Tam Samson’s dead!”

text:
I woke to find it already waking,
I rose, and it came after me;
I tried to cast it off in the crowd,
To lose it out on the sea,
source_text:
I waked, to find it first awake,
I rose, -- it followed me;
I tried to drop it in the crowd,
To lose it in the sea,

text:
Old John, with hair gone white,  
Laughs off his every worry, light,  
Seated beneath the oak tree’s shade,  
With all the old ones, softly stayed.  
They laugh to see us at our play,  
And then, in turn, they all will say,  
“Just so, just such were all the joys  
When we, once girls and boys,  
In youth were seen, long years ago,  
Upon the green where echoes go.”
source_text:
Old John, with white hair,
Does laugh away care,
Sitting under the oak,
Among the old folk.
They laugh at our play,
And soon they all say,
‘Such, such were the joys
When we all—girls and boys—
In our youth-time were seen
On the echoing green.’

text:
Set the flood loose, and you will see it plain,  
Surge upon surge, kept back for you;  
Scarlet proof! doubting Thomas,  
Now do you doubt your bird was true?
source_text:
Loose the flood, you shall find it patent,
Gush after gush, reserved for you;
Scarlet experiment! sceptic Thomas,
Now, do you doubt that your bird was true?

text:
Day after day, as the year runs down, the frosts  
Pluck at its green crown of leaves until it stands all bare;  
So upon those strange, assembled hosts  
Came Famine, a swift shadow, and the air  
Groaned under the weight of a fresh despair;  
Famine, whose deadlier child Misrule has never nursed  
At any of her thousand breasts, though sleeping there  
With sleepless eyes lie Faith, and Plague, and Slaughter,  
A grisly brood, conceived in Lethe’s sullen water.
source_text:
17.
Day after day, when the year wanes, the frosts
Strip its green crown of leaves, till all is bare;
So on those strange and congregated hosts
Came Famine, a swift shadow, and the air _3940
Groaned with the burden of a new despair;
Famine, than whom Misrule no deadlier daughter
Feeds from her thousand breasts, though sleeping there
With lidless eyes, lie Faith, and Plague, and Slaughter,
A ghastly brood; conceived of Lethe’s sullen water. _3945

text:
I had six oxen to my plough,
And they drew all right enough:
I sold them all, one after one—
Good ale keeps the heart above!
O good ale comes, &c.
source_text:
I had sax owsen in a pleugh,
And they drew a’ weel eneugh:
I sell’d them a’ just ane by ane—
Gude ale keeps the heart aboon!
O gude ale comes, &c.
